lede: Thon (protective / funerary rite) accounts for 29 catalogued manuscripts, 17 of them flagged research-priority. It clusters in Nan (55% of the corpus for this subject), ahead of Lampang and Phayao. Nearly all (93%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (90%) over palm-leaf · ใบลาน (10%). Dated witnesses run 1907–1937 CE (4 of 29 carry a date).

authored.title: Thon (protective / funerary rite)
authored.body_html:
What this is

Thon (ถอน — "to withdraw, pull out, annul") is the rite of un-doing: the recited ceremony that lifts a curse, dispels inauspiciousness and — above all — neutralises the danger left behind by a bad death. The catalogue holds 29 witnesses whose titles name it, and read together those titles draw the rite's purpose with unusual sharpness. Two words recur across them: _tai hong_ (the untimely, violent death) and _khuet_ (the taboo, the polluted or inauspicious). Thon phi tai hong, Thon khon tai, Thon tai ok luk (death in childbirth), Thon samanen tai (a novice's death); Thon sappa khuet (all inauspiciousness), Thon ban, Thon huean (of house and home), Kalakini (misfortune). The rite's verbs are _song_ — to send off, to dispatch — and _sut / suat_ — to recite.

Inference — the split in the catalogue between magic & ritual (14) and liturgy & chanting (7) is not confusion but the rite's actual nature: thon is magic performed as recitation. You do not draw it or brew it; you say it, and the saying is what withdraws the harm. That is why the same title so often pairs sut thon with katha and nyan — the spoken annulment carried alongside spell and diagram.

Beyond the catalogue — bad death and khuet

Tradition — the following is background from the wider Tai–Lanna tradition, not drawn from these manuscripts. In Thai and Lao folk belief a _tai hong_ death — violent, sudden, unnatural: accident, murder, drowning, and especially death in childbirth — is the most dangerous of all. The spirit of such a death does not settle; it becomes a _phi tai hong_, a restless and hungry ghost, and its danger can cling to the living, the house, even the village. Ordinary funerary merit is not enough; the death must be actively withdrawn — its pollution pulled out and sent away — before normal life can resume. _Khuet_ (ขึด) is the Lanna name for that broad field of taboo and inauspicious pollution: acts, places, times and events that carry misfortune and must be ritually cleared. Thon is the working answer to both. (This describes a well-attested belief-complex in general terms; I am not citing a specific source, and local practice varies.)

Inference — seen through that lens, the catalogue's title-lists stop looking like a jumble and become a taxonomy of dangerous deaths a practitioner had to be ready for: the layperson, the woman in childbirth, the novice, the monk, the ghost already gone bad — each with its own thon. The manuscript is a professional's index of crises.

The shape of the collection

Overwhelmingly a working paper technology — 26 mulberry-paper against just 3 palm-leaf — and almost entirely Tham Lanna (27 of 29). It leans research-priority (17 of 29, 59%) and clusters hard in Nan (16), with Lampang (6) and Phayao (4) behind — the vernacular-temple pattern, well away from the canon's Wat Sung Men gravity. Barely dated (4 witnesses, 1907–1937): late paper, in active use.

Notes

  • It travels inside the life-crisis kit. Thon rarely stands alone: the bundles bind it to suep cata life-extension, su khwan soul-calling, holasat almanac-reading and offering (Suep cata khao … Kam sut thon; Sut thon lae holasat). Steady the soul, stretch the span, and withdraw the harm — three moves of one repertoire.
  • Tradition — the pairing of thon with su khwan is telling: soul-calling gathers a scattered life-force in, thon sends danger out. Together they are the centripetal and centrifugal halves of the same care for a threatened person. (Inference from the two rites' logic, offered as interpretation.)
  • A living rite — a crawl target. High priority-density and still performed at Lanna deaths and misfortunes today, yet thinly represented in the digitized record: any practitioner's sut thon book — historical or current — is a primary source worth documenting.
